source_note: Transcribed from pedagogical_framework_specification.md and integrated_frameworks_by_band.md. Roles per stage: dominant | supporting | introduced | faded | retired. Stages: foundations (<=5.5), guided_control (6.0), independent_control (6.5-7.0), automatization (7.5+).

frameworks.name: Genre-Based Pedagogy
frameworks.purpose: Teach the learner what an IELTS response IS — deconstruct Band 8 models, co-write, then have the learner write alone through the teaching-learning cycle.
frameworks.theory: Genre theory (Systemic Functional Linguistics) — Hyland (2007); Martin & Rose (2008)
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner independently identifies and uses required genre moves

frameworks.name: Process Writing
frameworks.purpose: Enforce plan → draft → feedback → revise; revision of the learner's own work is mandatory before new prompts.
frameworks.theory: Cognitive process theory of writing — Flower & Hayes (1981); Badger & White (2000)
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Revision is measurably better than the first draft

frameworks.name: Focused Indirect Corrective Feedback
frameworks.purpose: Help the learner notice and repair recurring language errors — mark only 2-3 error categories; the learner self-corrects before direct correction is given.

frameworks.name: Scaffolding with Fading
frameworks.purpose: Systematically withdraw templates, sentence frames, and planning prompts as competence grows.
frameworks.theory: Zone of Proximal Development (Vygotsky, 1978) — Wood, Bruner & Ross (1976)

frameworks.name: Explicit Strategy Instruction
frameworks.purpose: Directly teach skimming, scanning, paraphrase recognition, and question-type strategies.
frameworks.theory: Strategy-based reading-comprehension research — Duke & Pearson (2002); Grabe (2009)
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner selects the correct strategy independently

frameworks.name: Reciprocal Teaching / Think-Aloud
frameworks.purpose: Verbalise reasoning through a question step by step, then hand the teacher role to the learner.

frameworks.name: Gradual Release of Responsibility
frameworks.purpose: I do → we do → you do progression for each question type, ending in timed exam conditions.
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner moves from modelled to independent performance

frameworks.name: Error-Driven Formative Diagnosis
frameworks.purpose: Classify every wrong answer by cause — vocabulary gap, paraphrase miss, trap, or location failure — and target the next drill accordingly.
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Repeated error causes reduce over time

frameworks.name: Process-Based Listening Instruction
frameworks.purpose: Diagnose why mishearing occurs; drill connected speech, weak forms, and word-boundary decoding.

frameworks.name: Metacognitive Pedagogical Cycle
frameworks.purpose: Predict → first listen → verify → second listen → reflect on what was missed and why.
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner can explain what was missed and why

frameworks.name: Micro-Listening and Dictation
frameworks.purpose: Sentence-level replay with dictation of exactly what was heard, making decoding gaps visible.

frameworks.name: Scaffolding with Fading (Listening)
frameworks.purpose: Make transcripts and replays available early in training, then systematically withdraw them as decoding accuracy stabilises.
frameworks.theory: Zone of Proximal Development (Vygotsky, 1978) — Wood, Bruner & Ross (1976)

frameworks.name: Task-Based Language Teaching
frameworks.purpose: Pre-task preparation → full uninterrupted task performance → post-task language analysis.
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner completes an uninterrupted task successfully

frameworks.name: Oral Corrective Feedback (Prompts)
frameworks.purpose: Signal the error and elicit self-repair; recasts reserved for errors above the learner's current level.
frameworks.theory: Output Hypothesis (Swain, 1995) — Lyster & Ranta (1997); Lyster & Saito (2010)
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Learner repairs spoken errors after prompts

frameworks.name: Fluency Development (4/3/2)
frameworks.purpose: Deliver the same talk in four, then three, then two minutes to proceduralise language.
frameworks.theory: Skill Acquisition Theory (DeKeyser, 2007) — Nation (1989); Nation & Newton (2009)
frameworks.evidence_of_progress: Output becomes faster, smoother, and less hesitant

frameworks.name: Reformulation
frameworks.purpose: Show a Band 8 version of the learner's own sentence; the learner then re-performs the upgraded version.

shared_spine.name: Backward Design
shared_spine.rule: Every activity must aim at a specific band descriptor. If asked why the Tutor is teaching this right now, the answer must be a band descriptor.
shared_spine.tightens_with_stage: The target descriptor moves up one rung as the learner improves.

shared_spine.name: Feedback Triad
shared_spine.rule: Every significant feedback message states: the goal, the current position, and one concrete next step. Maximum 2-3 priorities.
shared_spine.tightens_with_stage: The goal it points at climbs the descriptor scale.

shared_spine.name: Dynamic Assessment
shared_spine.rule: Weakest hint first; escalate one level at a time only if needed. Every hint is logged — hint level required is itself diagnostic data.
shared_spine.tightens_with_stage: Hints start weaker and are withdrawn sooner at higher stages.

shared_spine.name: Elicitation Before Telling
shared_spine.rule: The learner attempts, retrieves, identifies, or repairs before the Tutor reveals the answer.
shared_spine.tightens_with_stage: The elicitation gets vaguer — the learner is expected to do more of the finding independently.
